skill.name: shodan-reconnaissance
skill.description: Guides authorized Shodan reconnaissance for discovering exposed services, vulnerable hosts, internet-facing assets, and monitoring signals.
skill.summary: Plan and document authorized Shodan reconnaissance workflows for internet-facing assets.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static findings for Ruby backtick execution, weak crypto, certificate files, and generic secrets are mostly false positives caused by Markdown code fences, inline examples, and placeholders. The confirmed risk is dual-use Shodan reconnaissance guidance, including active scan submission, filtered vulnerable-host searches, result downloads, and API-key handling examples that require authorization and marketplace warnings.

medium_findings.title: Dual-use Shodan reconnaissance workflow
medium_findings.description: The skill teaches Shodan searches for exposed services, vulnerable hosts, webcams, industrial systems, open databases, and admin panels. This is legitimate for authorized security work, but it can also help identify third-party targets for misuse.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The semantic purpose and example queries directly support internet asset discovery and vulnerable service discovery. The file also states authorization requirements, so the risk is dual-use rather than confirmed malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: Active internet scan submission guidance
medium_findings.description: The guide includes commands for submitting Shodan on-demand scans against IP addresses and network ranges. Active scanning can affect external systems and should require verified authorization.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands explicitly submit scans and download scan results. The skill documents legal boundaries, which reduces malicious intent but does not remove operational risk.

medium_findings.title: External command examples can trigger network actions
medium_findings.description: The many shell examples are not executable by the skill itself, but users could run commands that query Shodan, download reconnaissance data, or submit scans. This confirms external command risk in documentation form, not embedded code execution.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The shell commands are visible in Markdown code blocks and call Shodan or curl endpoints. There is no evidence that the skill auto-runs them, so the confirmed risk is user-directed command execution.

low_findings.title: API key placeholders require secret-handling caution
low_findings.description: The skill uses placeholder API keys in Shodan CLI, REST API, and Python examples. No real secret is present, but users may paste real keys into shell history or scripts if they follow the examples directly.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The values are placeholders rather than embedded credentials. The risk is user mishandling of real Shodan keys when adapting the examples.

low_findings.title: Sudo package installation example
low_findings.description: The installation section includes a sudo package manager command. It is a normal documented install path, but elevated installation commands should be reviewed before execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The sudo command is present and easy to verify. It installs a named distribution package and is not evidence of privilege escalation by the skill itself.

low_findings.title: Static scanner false positives from Markdown examples
low_findings.description: Most Ruby backtick, weak cryptography, certificate-file, hardcoded IP, and generic secret findings are Markdown examples or search filters. No executable Ruby code, cryptographic implementation, certificate file access, or real credential was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review shows these patterns occur in Markdown code blocks, tables, placeholders, or Shodan query syntax. No evidence of hidden scripts or automatic execution was found.

dangerous_patterns.title: Vulnerable target discovery queries
dangerous_patterns.description: The guide includes Shodan queries for vulnerable RDP, Log4j, exposed databases, open VNC, cameras, industrial systems, and admin panels. These queries are valuable for defense but can enable unauthorized target selection.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The listed queries clearly enumerate exposed or vulnerable internet services. Their use depends on authorization and intent.

dangerous_patterns.title: Bulk reconnaissance data export
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill shows how to download Shodan result sets and parse them into CSV or other outputs. Bulk exports can support legitimate asset inventory or unauthorized collection.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The download and parse workflows are explicit. The commands are documentation examples, but they enable bulk collection if executed.

content.value_statement: Security teams need a repeatable way to understand internet-exposed assets and risky services. This skill provides authorized Shodan workflows for discovery, reporting, and monitoring.
actual_capabilities: Explains Shodan CLI setup, account checks, and API key initialization.
Provides host lookup, search, count, statistics, download, and parse workflows.
Lists Shodan filters for networks, geography, organizations, products, vulnerabilities, screenshots, and SSL certificates.
Documents on-demand scan submission, scan status checks, and scan result retrieval.
Shows REST API and Python library examples for automated Shodan lookups.
Summarizes credit costs, rate limits, data freshness limits, and authorization requirements.
limitations: Requires a Shodan account and available query or scan credits.
Does not verify ownership or authorization for targets by itself.
Shodan data may be stale unless on-demand scans are authorized and available.
Does not include exploit execution, vulnerability validation, or remediation automation.

use_cases.title: External Asset Inventory
use_cases.description: Identify known internet-facing hosts, ports, services, and products for an authorized organization.

use_cases.title: Penetration Test Scoping
use_cases.description: Prepare Shodan searches and exports that support authorized reconnaissance before manual validation.

use_cases.title: Exposure Monitoring
use_cases.description: Use Shodan monitoring concepts to track new services, vulnerabilities, and certificate changes.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Basic Lookup
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner workflow for one authorized host.
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me plan a Shodan lookup for an IP address I own. Include the CLI steps, expected output fields, and safe handling notes.

prompt_templates.title: Build an Organization Query Set
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate workflow for authorized organization reconnaissance.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Shodan query plan for my authorized organization. Cover organization, hostname, port, product, vulnerability, and certificate filters.

prompt_templates.title: Design an Export Workflow
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate reporting workflow for asset inventory.
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a Shodan export and parsing workflow for authorized assets. Include fields for IP, port, hostnames, product, version, and organization.

prompt_templates.title: Review an Exposure Assessment
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my authorized Shodan findings and propose a prioritized exposure assessment. Include risk categories, validation steps, and remediation owners.

output_examples.input: Plan a Shodan review for an owned web server.
output_examples.output: The response outlines account checks, host lookup, relevant service fields, screenshot availability, vulnerability filters, and a short authorization reminder.

output_examples.input: Create a query plan for an authorized company domain.
output: Start with hostname and organization filters.
Add product, port, and certificate filters for focused review.
Download only the needed result set and track credit use.
Validate findings with approved internal records before reporting.

output_examples.input: Summarize Shodan exposure findings for a security manager.
output_examples.output: The response groups exposed services by risk, lists likely owners, calls out stale data concerns, and recommends verification before remediation.

best_practices: Use the skill only for assets you own or are explicitly authorized to assess.
Store Shodan API keys in approved secret managers or protected environment configuration.
Confirm Shodan results with internal inventory before reporting a vulnerability.
anti_patterns: Do not search for random exposed webcams, databases, or industrial systems without authorization.
Do not submit on-demand scans for third-party IP addresses or ranges.
Do not paste real API keys into shared prompts, tickets, screenshots, or shell history.

faq.question: Does this skill run Shodan commands automatically?
faq.answer: No. It is a written guide with command examples that a user may choose to run.

faq.question: Is authorization required?
faq.answer: Yes. Use it only for assets you own or have written permission to assess.

faq.question: Does it need a paid Shodan plan?
faq.answer: Some workflows can use a free account, but filtered searches, downloads, monitoring, and scans may require credits or paid features.

faq.question: Can it validate vulnerabilities?
faq.answer: No. It identifies Shodan-reported exposure and vulnerability signals that require separate validation.

faq.question: How should API keys be handled?
faq.answer: Use approved secret storage and avoid placing real keys in prompts, public files, or command history.

faq.question: Why is this skill marked medium risk?
faq.answer: It is legitimate for authorized security work, but the same Shodan workflows can identify vulnerable third-party systems.
